title: [PDF] THE TRANS–AUSTRALIAN RAILWAY - Engineers Australia
content:
### Survey Leadership and Logistics (1904–1909)

| Party | Leader | Personnel and Animals | Timeline |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Western Australian Party | Richard Anketell (engineering surveyor) | "three other surveyors, ten camelmen and assistants and 91 camels" | "began in mid-1904"; "reached the border" by "the end of September, after only three months in the field" |
| South Australian Party | J.T. Furner | Not specified | "left Port Augusta in June 1908"; reached "the border cairn in March 1909" |

### Survey Conduct and Instruments
*   **Alignment setting:** "Anketell set the alignment by compass, checking it by stellar observations"
*   **Route marking:** "marking the route by means of a heavy ‘snigging chain’ drawn by one of his camels"
*   **Follow-up party:** "The chaining, pegging and leveling party followed."
*   **Water logistics:** "Pack camels left 270 litre caches of water every eleven kilometres"
*   **Camps:** "at each of these [water caches], a camel wagon party established an overnight camp."
*   **Border mark:** The Western Australian party "built a cairn" at the border.
*   **Theodolite:** The term "theodolite" is not mentioned in the provided text.

### Measurement of the Straight Section
*   **Method:** The text does not explicitly describe a separate measurement process for the straight section beyond the general survey methods of setting alignment "by compass, checking it by stellar observations" and using a "‘snigging chain’," "chaining, pegging and leveling party."
*   **Length:** "longest straight stretch of track in the world (478 km 193 m)"
*   **Reference to 297 miles:** The specific figure "297 miles" is not mentioned in the provided text.

### Missing References
*   **Mile 496:** There is no mention of "Mile 496" in the provided text.
*   **Mile 793:** There is no mention of "Mile 793" in the provided text.
